paper.title_en: A Bayesian reappraisal for quantification of signal detection in IACT: An application to MACE data analysis
paper.abstract_en: Imaging Atmospheric Cherenkov Telescopes (IACTs) in γ-ray astronomy have long relied on the frequentist 5σ significance threshold for detecting γ-ray sources. However, the frequentist hypothesis starts by assuming the null hypothesis (H0) is true and assigns the probability of the data observed under H0, i.e. P(data|H0), instead of providing the probability that H0 is true. But analytically, to quantify the exactness of a hypothesis ‘H’ supported by observed data, one must quantify the P(H|data). But according to Bayes’ theorem, for any hypothesis ‘H’, these probabilities are not the same (i.e. P(data|H) ≠ P(H|data)). This work proposes a Bayesian alternative for quantifying signal detection in gamma-ray astronomy using IACTs. We demonstrate that switching to the Bayesian paradigm offers a mathematically consistent and comprehensive alternative to the conventional 5σ standard for source detection in ground-based γ-ray astronomy. We applied the Bayesian method to simulate integral sensitivities for MAGIC and MACE telescopes.This study shows that the Bayesian threshold (Bayes Factor ≥ 103), unlike the frequentist 5σ significance threshold, represents a uniform scale of belief for the alternative hypothesis H1 over H0, for a particular IACT. In addition to redefining the sensitivity of an IACT, the Bayesian formalism also helps improve integral sensitivities, as demonstrated on the MAGIC and MACE telescopes using reported signal and background rates.
